source: Consumer Protection (Jurisdiction of the District Commission, the State Commission and the National Commission) Rules, 2021, notified by the Department of Consumer Affairs.
disclaimer: Pecuniary limits have changed multiple times since 2019 and may change again. Verify current limits via the Department of Consumer Affairs notifications before filing.

pecuniary_thresholds.comment: District: up to Rs. 50 lakh (50,00,000). State: above Rs. 50 lakh up to Rs. 2 crore (2,00,00,000). National: above Rs. 2 crore.

territorial_rules.comment: Per Section 34 of CPA 2019, a complaint may be filed before the District Commission where (a) the opposite party resides or carries on business or has a branch office, OR (b) the cause of action wholly or in part arises, OR (c) the complainant resides or personally works for gain (introduced by CPA 2019 — a significant pro-consumer change vs. the 1986 Act).

filing_fee_indicative.comment: Indicative filing fees per common practice (verify current schedule with your State's Consumer Commission rules). Some states waive fees up to small claim thresholds.

e_filing.comment: e-Daakhil is the National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission's online filing portal. It has been progressively rolled out to State and District Commissions across India.
